meta.title: Consolidated Solar Battery System
meta.subtitle: Design & Build Specification — Rev 3.2
meta.location: Solariver Salvage — Salt Lake City Outdoor Installation

overview.battery_chemistry: LiFePO4 (lithium iron phosphate) — Narada 105Ah prismatic cells
overview.cell_configuration: 4S×P scalable — 26× Narada 105Ah new cells; initial build 4S2P (8 cells), expandable to 4S6P (24 cells) in main unit or via satellite units
overview.usable_capacity: ~6,451 Wh at 80% DoD (full 4S6P build); ~2,150 Wh initial 4S2P
overview.solar_inputs: 2× 40W + 1× 25W (EPever Tracer 2210AN MPPT, one per pump) + 2× 100W Renogy RNG-100D-SS (4th EPever Tracer 2210AN MPPT) = 305W total
overview.thermal_mgmt: Active — thermostat-controlled heat + internal fan recirculation
overview.design_load: ~65W average (all pumps) + up to 20W thermal management
overview.autonomy: 99+ hours / 4.1 days (full 4S6P, pump load only); 3.2 days including worst-case thermal
overview.installation: Salt Lake City, outdoors, freestanding boulder enclosure

rows: 2× 100W Renogy RNG-100D-SS, Vmp 20.4V, Voc 24.3V — via 4th EPever Tracer 2210AN MPPT

content.body:
All three pump controllers are confirmed to operate without a battery, powered directly from the solar panel. They accept any clean 12V supply without proprietary handshaking. The consolidated 12.8V LiFePO4 bank replaces all three original 12V battery packs with 23–77× the combined capacity (initial 4S2P through full 4S6P build).

Two additional 100W Renogy RNG-100D-SS panels (found in storage) are integrated as a dedicated charging source via a 4th EPever Tracer 2210AN MPPT controller. The panels run in parallel (20.4V Vmp, ~190W effective) into the MPPT which charges the battery bank only — no load output used for this controller. The included Renogy Wanderer PWM controller is retained as a backup spare.

During daylight hours with solar available, the EPever MPPT controllers' load output terminals power the pump controllers directly from solar, bypassing the battery for the pump load. The battery only receives net surplus power after the pump load is satisfied. This reduces battery cycling and extends cell life significantly.
On a typical clear SLC day with 5.5 peak sun hours, solar harvests approximately 578 Wh net after powering the pumps.

rows: Solar offset — clear day (5.5 peak hrs)
Net battery draw — fully cloudy day

content.body: With 6,451 Wh usable capacity (8,064 Wh nominal at 80% DoD, full 4S6P build):

items: Fully cloudy days: 6,451 ÷ 2,244 = 2.9 days autonomy
With 305W solar on a clear day (1,677 Wh harvest): system is solar-positive, battery gains ~117 Wh net
Consecutive cloudy days: ~99 hours / 4.1 days at pump-only load without any solar input
Initial 4S2P build (2,150 Wh usable): ~33 hours autonomy — sufficient for commissioning and testing
Each satellite expansion unit adds one 4S string (105Ah / 1,344 Wh nominal) by connecting leads only

content.body: The 12.8V nominal LiFePO4 bank (4S configuration) directly matches all three pump controllers 12V input requirements. No boost or step-up converters are needed. The three salvaged 18V solar panels each feed a dedicated EPever Tracer 2210AN MPPT controller for pump pass-through and battery charging. The two additional 100W Renogy panels (Vmp 20.4V) feed a 4th EPever Tracer 2210AN MPPT for battery charging only — no load output wired on this controller.

EPever Tracer MPPT controllers include dedicated load output terminals that intelligently manage solar pass-through. When the battery is full and solar is available, pump load is powered directly from solar. When solar is insufficient, the battery supplements or takes over automatically.

items: Set load output mode to 'Normal' or 'Always On' in EPever settings
Confirm load output current rating covers your pump draw (Tracer 2210AN: 20A load output)
Wire all three pump controller inputs to the load output terminals of their respective MPPT controllers

content.body:
Solariver panels (3× 18V) → EPever Tracer 2210AN MPPT (3×, isolated inputs) → load output terminals → pump controllers (3× 12V). Simultaneously: MPPT charge outputs → 12.8V LiFePO4 bank via 2× Smart BMS → distribution bus → fused 12V taps → pump controllers (battery backup). Renogy panels (2× 100W, Vmp 20.4V, parallel) → 4th EPever Tracer 2210AN MPPT → battery bank (charging only). The two Smart BMS units each manage one 4S parallel sub-bank; outputs combine at the positive bus. 80A ANL fuse on the battery positive bus, close to the cells. LEM HASS 200-S current sensor inline on the main bus for system-wide monitoring.

Satellite battery units connect to the main bus via heavy leads only. Each satellite contains 4 cells (one 4S string) plus active thermal management (heater + fan + thermostat). No charge controllers, BMS, or monitoring electronics are installed in satellite enclosures. BMS balance/sense wires extend from the main unit to each satellite string.

rows: 8 cells initial (4S2P); expandable to 24 active + 2 spare (4S6P)
4S 100A Smart LiFePO4 BMS with low-temp cutoff
Each manages one 4S sub-bank; Bluetooth module attached
Salvaged from Solariver kits — load output terminals essential
EPever Tracer 2210AN (4th unit), max PV 50V, 20A charge
For 2× 100W Renogy panels in parallel — charging only, no load output used. ~190W effective harvest.
On positive bus, close to battery terminal
Battery to bus + satellite unit leads
Bus to pump controllers; BMS sense wires to satellite units

content.body: LiFePO4 is strongly preferred over LiPo for this application. LiPo cells perform poorly in cold temperatures, require more careful management to avoid thermal runaway, and have significantly shorter cycle lives (300–500 cycles vs 2,000+ for LiFePO4). For an unattended outdoor installation in Salt Lake City's climate extremes, LiFePO4's chemical stability, cold-temperature tolerance, and long service life make it the clear choice.

The purchased cells are new Narada 3.2V 105Ah 336Wh LiFePO4 prismatic cells — not surplus or Grade B stock. Narada is a reputable Chinese LiFePO4 manufacturer. At $30/cell for new cells this represents excellent value. Each cell is rated 105Ah (336Wh); 24 cells in 4S6P yield 630Ah / 8,064Wh nominal — comparable to the original 200Ah 4S3P design at 7,680Wh, with the added benefit of scalability and two spare cells.

With 26 cells purchased and 24 needed for full 4S6P, two cells remain as tested spares. Measure and record actual capacity of all 26 cells before assignment. Any cell measuring significantly below 105Ah should be reserved as a spare or placed in a lower-priority string position.

26× Narada 105Ah 3.2V LiFePO4 prismatic cells in scalable 4S×P configuration:
4S = 4 cells in series = 12.8V nominal. ×P = number of parallel strings.
Initial build: 4S2P (8 cells, 210Ah, 2,688Wh nominal, ~2,150Wh usable)
Full main-unit build: 4S6P (24 cells, 630Ah, 8,064Wh nominal, ~6,451Wh usable) + 2 spare cells
Satellite expansion: each satellite unit adds one 4S string (4 cells, +105Ah, +1,344Wh nominal)
Total bank: 12.8V × (105Ah × number of parallel strings)

items: 4S = 4 cells in series per string = 12.8V nominal
×P = number of parallel strings (1–6 in main unit; additional via satellite units)
Initial build: 4S2P = 210Ah, 2,688 Wh nominal
Full main-unit build: 4S6P = 630Ah, 8,064 Wh nominal
Satellite expansion: plug in additional 4S strings via leads — no extra electronics required
Two spare cells remain after full 4S6P build — capacity-test and store for replacement

content.body: Cell dimensions: measure actual Narada 105Ah cells before finalizing enclosure design. Approximate dimensions ~175mm × 60mm × 158mm (verify against physical cells). Enclosure design requires significant revision from original 200Ah cell spec.

For any ×P configuration, cells at the same series position across parallel strings should be matched. Capacity-test all 26 cells individually. Sort into groups where cells at the same series position are within 2% of each other. With 26 cells and 4 series positions, select the best-matching 6 per position for the full 4S6P build; the remaining 2 become spares.

Unlike 2P where you match pairs, 3P matching is harder — you need three cells to agree, not two. Buying exactly 12 cells for a 12-cell pack leaves no matching margin. Consider purchasing 13–14 cells if budget allows, to ensure four clean triplets.
These constraints are enforced by the BMS and thermostat system and must never be bypassed.

items: Charging prohibited below 0°C (32°F) — causes lithium plating and permanent cell damage
Discharging acceptable down to −20°C (−4°F) at reduced current
Optimal operating range: 10°C to 35°C (50°F to 95°F)
Maximum charge temperature: 45°C (113°F)
BMS charge-inhibit port wired to temperature probe — enforces 0°C charge floor automatically

content.body: SLC winters regularly reach −15°F (−26°C) and summers push 105°F (40°C+). The approach is insulation-first, with active thermostat-controlled heating and internal fan recirculation within the 20W power budget. The larger 4S3P pack provides additional thermal mass, which slows temperature changes and reduces heater cycling in winter.

A 12V PTC ceramic heater element (10–15W) inside the battery zone, wired to the thermostat heating relay. PTC elements are self-regulating — resistance increases as temperature rises, naturally throttling power draw.

items: Heater ON below +5°C (41°F)
Heater OFF at +10°C (50°F)
BMS charge inhibit released only above 0°C (32°F)
Typical draw: 10–15W active, self-throttles as setpoint approaches

content.body: Internal recirculation via a 12V brushless fan (3–5W) mounted in the electronics zone. The sealed enclosure has no external vents — the fan circulates air between the battery and electronics zones to equalize temperatures. LiFePO4 tolerates 45°C during discharge, providing meaningful headroom on SLC's hottest days.

items: Fan ON above +35°C (95°F) inside battery zone
Fan OFF below +30°C (86°F)

rows: MPPT charge output disconnected via BMS temp port

content.body: Controller: STC-1000 dual-stage thermostat (~$15). Single unit manages both heating and cooling relays with one temperature probe mounted against the cell surface inside the battery zone.

ENCLOSURE REDESIGN IN PROGRESS (Rev 3.2): Cell change from 200Ah to Narada 105Ah and adoption of a modular main-unit + satellite architecture requires significant updates to all enclosure dimensions, cell stack layout, and internal zone assignments. STL/DXF files from Rev 3.1 are superseded. Measure actual Narada 105Ah cells before designing new enclosures.

The system uses a modular two-tier enclosure architecture:

MAIN UNIT: One boulder-style three-shell enclosure housing all electronics (2× Smart BMS, 3× EPever MPPT, 1× Renogy Wanderer PWM, distribution bus, fuses, LEM HASS current sensor, thermostat/heater/fan) plus the initial 4S2P cell bank (expandable to 4S6P within the main unit). All control electronics and monitoring live here.

SATELLITE UNITS: Minimal boulder-style enclosures housing additional 4S cell strings only. Internal hardware limited to: cells, heater element, recirculation fan, and thermostat. Connect to main unit via heavy positive/negative leads. No BMS, no charge controllers, no monitoring. Add satellite units to expand capacity without touching main unit electronics.

Both unit types use the same three-shell ASA/PETG construction. Satellite units are smaller (4 cells vs up to 24 in main unit) and have no dedicated electronics zone.

Design principle: Foam is only structural/thermal. No wires, no components, and no connections exist inside the foam cavity. Everything serviceable lives inside the inner service shell, accessed by removing the bolted top lid.

rows: Cosmetic, weather barrier, structural outer wall. ASA required for UV resistance. Print in enclosed chamber (Prusa Core One).
Insulation + structural rigidity between shells 1 and 2
Inner wall of foam cavity, structural support. No UV exposure (inside foam). PETG chosen over ASA for superior layer adhesion and minimal warping on large parts.
Yes — bonded to outer shell before foam pour
Houses ALL components: cells, BMS, electronics, thermal system. PETG provides excellent layer adhesion for structural integrity. 80°C HDT gives 30°C+ margin over worst-case internal temp.
Bolted to Shell 2 — removable lid access from top

content.body: The foam cavity exists only between the outer boulder shell and the middle structural shell. It contains no components, no wiring, and no connections. The foam is injected after shells 1 (ASA) and 2 (PETG) are aligned with printed tabs and clamped, with all cable conduit sleeves in place. The expanding pour foam bonds mechanically to both shell surfaces, permanently locking them together — no solvent welding needed between the dissimilar materials. Cable conduits are printed PETG tubes that pass through the foam wall — the foam bonds around the outside of the conduit, but the conduit interior remains hollow and clear, allowing wires to be pulled through at any time.

items: Foam cavity: 25–40mm between shell 1 (outer) and shell 2 (middle)
Printed PETG conduit sleeves installed before foam pour — one per cable penetration
Conduit inner diameter sized for cable + PG gland (PG9 or PG13.5)
Foam is poured through injection ports in shell 1 after conduits are verified clear
This step IS irreversible — but nothing serviceable lives in the foam cavity
After foam cures, the outer structure is rigid, insulated, and weatherproof

content.body: All serviceable components mount inside the inner service shell, which is attached to the middle structural shell (shell 2) with bolted connections — not solvent-welded. Full top-down access to all components is provided by removing the bolted lid from the cap rim. No side-wall openings are needed.

rows: 12× LiFePO4 cells (4S3P), BMS, PTC heater, temp probe
Remove lid — full top-down access to cell stack and BMS
3× MPPT controllers, bus bars, fuse block, thermostat, fan
Remove lid — full top-down access to electronics DIN rail and bus bars

content.body: Every component mounts mechanically to the inner service shell or to DIN rail / mounting plates bolted to it. No component is glued, foamed, or permanently bonded.

rows: Compression hardware (threaded rod + end plates) sitting on a printed shelf/cradle in battery zone
Unbolt compression hardware, lift cells out through top
Bolted to mounting plate or DIN rail clip in battery zone
Disconnect balance leads and power, unbolt, lift out through top
Bolted bracket or thermal-adhesive pad on mounting plate (not on cell surface directly)
Unplug from thermostat relay, unbolt bracket
Kapton tape to cell surface — not permanent
Peel tape, reroute probe through conduit
DIN rail mount or bolted to standoffs in electronics zone
Disconnect PV/battery/load terminals, unclip from DIN rail
Bolted to insulated standoffs in electronics zone
Bolted to mounting plate in electronics zone
DIN rail clip in electronics zone
Unplug probe and relay connections, unclip
Bolted to bracket or standoffs in electronics zone
Routed through PETG conduit sleeves that pass through foam cavity
Disconnect at both ends, pull through conduit. New wire feeds back through the same conduit.

content.body: All cables between the exterior (solar panels, pumps) and the inner service shell pass through printed PETG conduit sleeves embedded in the foam cavity. The conduits are installed before the foam pour. Their inner diameter is sized so cables (with connectors removed) can be pulled through freely. PG cable glands thread into the outer end of each conduit for weather sealing.

items: Main bus conduit: 1× large conduit (ID ~20mm) for 8 AWG battery bus runs between zones
Solar conduits: 3× conduits (ID ~12mm each) for MC4/panel wiring from exterior to MPPT zone
Pump conduits: 3× conduits (ID ~10mm each) for load output wiring to external pump controllers
Sensor conduit: 1× small conduit (ID ~8mm) for thermostat probe and any monitoring wiring
All conduits terminate with PG cable glands at the outer shell for IP68 weather sealing
Conduit interiors remain permanently clear — foam bonds around the outside only

content.body: A structural ASA cap rim spans all three shells at the top of the enclosure. Shell 3 is placed first, then the cap is lowered on top with its inner lip entering Shell 3. Foam is poured (step 9) to bond the cap permanently — no mechanical fasteners to the shells. A foam flange extends 20mm into the foam cavity, providing a large bonding surface. Brass M4 heat-set inserts (OD ~5.6mm, length 8mm) are embedded in the foam flange zone; the expanding foam bonds around them for secondary anchoring. The cap's outer lip overhangs Shell 1 by 10mm and drops 15mm down the outer wall. An inner lip (5mm overhang, 15mm drop) extends inside Shell 3, capturing it from within.

rows: Shell 1 outer (692 × 417) + 10mm lip on each side
Inside Shell 3 wall + inner lip overhang
Flat top surface — dual gasket grooves and bolt holes
Brass inserts embedded here — foam bonds around them
Drops inside Shell 3 — captures Shell 3 from inside
Outer + inner grooves with bolt pattern between them
Stainless socket head cap screws through lid into brass inserts
UV resistant — cap top surface is exterior-exposed

content.body: The removable lid is a flat 712 × 437 × 5mm ASA panel with M4 clearance holes matching the cap's insert pattern. Two 3D-printed TPU gaskets (4mm wide × 2mm thick, ~95A Shore hardness) sit in the cap's dual grooves — one outside the bolt pattern, one inside — and are compressed when the lid is bolted down. This dual-seal arrangement provides redundant water sealing. To service any component, unbolt the lid (22× M4 bolts), lift it off, and you have full top-down access to the interior through the 594 × 319mm opening.

items: Cap rim printed in ASA (UV-exposed top surface). Sand contact surfaces before foam pour to improve mechanical bond.
Lid printed flat in ASA — single piece if printer bed allows, otherwise segmented and solvent-welded.
TPU gaskets are printed components (95A Shore) — two separate loops, each printed as a continuous spiral that fits on one print bed. Press-fit into cap grooves. Reprint if damaged during service.
Assembly order: Shell 3 placed inside Shell 2 first, then cap lowered on top (inner lip enters Shell 3). Foam poured to bond cap (step 9). Lid bolted last (step 11).

content.body: Shell 1 (outer boulder) printed in ASA for UV resistance — the only component requiring ASA. Apply stone-texture spray paint (Rust-Oleum Stone or similar) to match existing landscape features. UV-resistant clear topcoat protects finish and reduces solar heat gain. Light/reflective color is important — a dark boulder in SLC summer sun would significantly increase thermal load on the system.

Flat printed base with four heat-set M8 threaded inserts for optional anchor bolts into concrete or patio surface. System weight approximately 28–33kg (cells alone ~24kg) — inherently stable freestanding. Optional printed anchor skirt with landscape stake holes for high-wind environments.

rows: Test Voc and Isc before integration.

items.title: Test salvaged panels
items.body: Measure Voc and Isc on each panel in direct sun. Expected: ~21–22V Voc, 1–2.3A Isc depending on wattage.

Panel 2 (40W) — Voc and Isc measured

items.title: Grade and test LiFePO4 cells
items.body: Capacity-test all 12 cells individually. Match into four parallel triplets within 2% capacity. Any cell outside tolerance becomes a spare.
checkpoints: All 12 cells individually capacity-tested
Cells sorted by capacity

items.title: Build and bench-test battery pack
items.body: Wire 4S3P, connect BMS, verify cell voltages balance, confirm BMS protection triggers. The 3P configuration requires bus bars connecting three parallel cells per series group.
checkpoints: 4S3P wired with compression hardware
Bus bars connecting parallel triplets installed
BMS connected — balance leads + power
Cell group voltages balance within 20mV
BMS over-discharge cutoff test passed
BMS over-temp simulation test passed
Pack voltage reads ~12.8–13.2V

items.title: Build and bench-test full electronics stack
items.body: Wire EPever MPPT controllers, distribution bus, fuse block, and thermostat. Verify solar pass-through and thermal controls.
checkpoints: All 3 MPPTs programmed with LiFePO4 settings
MPPT battery terminals connected to bus bars
Fuse block wired with correct fuses (2× 5A, 1× 3A)
Solar pass-through verified on load output
All 3 pumps run from MPPT load output
Thermostat heater ON/OFF at correct temps
Thermostat fan ON/OFF at correct temps
BMS charge inhibit activates below 0°C

items.title: 24-hour bench endurance test
items.body: Run all pumps for 24 hours without solar. Target: >3,900 Wh remaining (>50% SOC with 4S3P pack).
checkpoints: Start SOC and voltage recorded
24-hour test completed without interruption
End SOC and voltage recorded
Remaining capacity >3,900 Wh (>50% SOC)

items.title: Design three-shell enclosure in CAD
items.body: Design the three-shell boulder: outer shell (cosmetic), middle shell (foam cavity inner wall), and inner service shell. Design cable conduit sleeves for all penetrations through the foam cavity. Design cap rim (spans all three shells, bonds during foam pour) and removable lid. Segment all shells for Prusa Core One bed.
checkpoints: Cell stack dimensions measured and documented
Electronics stack dimensions measured
Three-shell boulder designed in CAD
Foam cavity 25–40mm between shell 1 and shell 2
Cable conduit sleeves designed (correct ID per cable run)
Cap rim designed — outer lip, foam flange, inner lip profiled
Brass insert positions marked in cap foam flange zone (22× M4)
Dual gasket grooves (2× 4mm × 2mm) routed in cap top surface
Lid designed — flat panel with M4 clearance holes matching cap
All shells segmented for Prusa bed size

items.title: Print shells and conduit sleeves
items.body: Print Shell 1 (outer boulder) and cap rim in ASA for UV resistance — enclosed chamber required (Prusa Core One). Print Shell 2, Shell 3, conduit sleeves, and all internal brackets in PETG for superior layer adhesion and minimal warping. Print lid in ASA (UV-exposed). Print two TPU gaskets (95A Shore) as separate spiral loops on one print bed. PETG's 80°C HDT provides ample margin for internal temperatures.
checkpoints: Outer boulder shell sections printed (ASA, enclosed chamber)
Cap rim sections printed (ASA) — sand foam-contact surfaces
Lid sections printed (ASA) — flat panel, segmented if needed
TPU gaskets printed (95A Shore) — 2 spiral loops for dual grooves
Middle structural shell sections printed (PETG)
Inner service shell sections printed (PETG)
All cable conduit sleeves printed (PETG) and test-fit
Heat-set brass M4 inserts installed in cap foam flange (22×)

items.title: Assemble outer structure and pour foam
items.body: Align Shell 1 (ASA, outer) and Shell 2 (PETG, middle) with printed alignment tabs and temporary clamps or screws. These are different materials and cannot be solvent-welded — the expanding pour foam bonds mechanically to both surfaces. Position all conduit sleeves between shells before pouring. Place the cap rim on top — lip resting over Shell 1 outer edge, foam flange hanging into the cavity with brass inserts. Verify all conduits are clear with pull wires. Pour foam — it bonds cap, shells, and conduits permanently. This is the only irreversible step — but no components live in the foam cavity.
checkpoints: Shell 1 (ASA) and Shell 2 (PETG) aligned with tabs/clamps
All cable conduit sleeves (PETG) positioned and secured
Pull wire passed through EVERY conduit — all clear
All conduit ends capped to prevent foam entry
Cap rim placed on top — lip over Shell 1, foam flange into cavity
Brass inserts in cap flange verified positioned correctly
Foam injected through injection ports in Shell 1
24-hour foam cure observed — foam locks shells + cap together
Post-cure: all conduits still clear (pull wire test)
Post-cure: cap rim firmly bonded — no movement

items.title: Install inner service shell and mount components
items.body: Shell 3 is already in position (placed before cap in step 9). Cap's inner lip captures Shell 3 from inside. Bolt Shell 3 to Shell 2 (not welded). Mount all components mechanically — no glue, no foam. Route cables through conduit sleeves. Every component is accessible by lifting out through the top opening.
checkpoints: Shell 3 already in position (placed before cap) — captured by inner lip
Inner service shell bolted to middle shell
Cell compression cradle installed in battery zone
12 cells installed with compression hardware
BMS bolted to mounting plate or DIN rail
PTC heater mounted on bracket (bolted, not glued)
Temp probe on cell surface with kapton tape
MPPT controllers on DIN rail or standoffs
Bus bars on insulated standoffs
Fuse block mounted
STC-1000 thermostat installed
Fan mounted on bracket in electronics zone
All cables routed through conduit sleeves
PG cable glands threaded into conduit outer ends
Full electrical test BEFORE installing lid

items.title: Install lid and seal enclosure
items.body: Place two 3D-printed TPU gaskets into cap rim's dual grooves (4mm wide × 2mm deep each — one outer, one inner). Lower lid onto cap, aligning bolt holes. Insert M4 × 16mm stainless socket head cap screws through lid clearance holes into cap brass inserts. Torque evenly in a star pattern to compress both gaskets uniformly.
checkpoints: TPU gaskets seated in both cap grooves — continuous loops, no gaps
Lid placed on cap — all 22 bolt holes aligned
M4 bolts inserted and hand-tightened in star pattern
Final torque applied evenly — both gaskets compressed uniformly
Visual check: no gaps between lid and cap around perimeter

items.title: Finish and install
items.body: Apply stone-texture paint and UV topcoat. Place in position, connect solar panels and pumps, verify operation. Confirm serviceability by unbolting lid, verifying full top-down access, and re-sealing.
checkpoints: Stone texture paint applied (light color)
UV-resistant clear topcoat applied
Boulder placed in final position
Solar panels connected — charging confirmed
All pump controllers connected and running
Serviceability test: unbolt lid, verify top-down access, re-seal
Full system operational verification complete

content.body: Confirmed purchased total: $953.72 (cells $780 + BMS $79.98 + Bluetooth $7.99 + current sensor $2.00 + busbars $26.00 + MC4 $14.00 + fish paper $43.75). Salvaged/storage items: $0. Additional to purchase: 1× EPever Tracer 2210AN (~$40–55). Remaining estimated: $440–755 per enclosure for MPPT + filament, foam, hardware, thermal components, and wiring. Two enclosures (main unit + first satellite) estimated $880–1,510 additional spend.
Review ALL safety notes before beginning any electrical work. LiFePO4 cells at 200Ah can deliver thousands of amps into a short circuit.

items: Never short cell terminals — even briefly. Cover all terminals with tape when not actively being connected.
Always connect the BMS before connecting any load or charge source to the pack.
Do not charge below 0°C — the thermostat/BMS interlock exists for this reason. Never bypass it.
The foam pour (step 9) is irreversible — but no components live in the foam cavity. Verify all conduit sleeves are clear with pull wires before and after foam pour.
Every component must remain removable by lifting out through the top. Never glue, foam, or permanently bond any electrical component to the enclosure.
Test thermostat, heater, and fan independently during step 10 before installing lid in step 11.
The ANL fuse on the positive bus is your primary fault protection. Install within 30cm of battery positive terminal.
When disposing of old Solariver batteries, take to a battery recycling facility.
The 4S3P pack stores 7,680 Wh — significantly more energy than the original 4S2P design. Treat it with proportional respect.
With 3P parallel strings, connect all three cells in each group simultaneously. Never connect a charged cell in parallel with a discharged cell.

content.body: All four Tracer 2210AN controllers must be programmed with identical LiFePO4 battery settings. The 4th controller (Renogy 100W panels) does not use its load output terminals — set Load Working Mode to OFF or leave unconnected.

rows: Select USER then set voltages below

content.body: Temperature Compensation must be set to 0 mV/°C/2V for LiFePO4. The default lead-acid compensation will overcharge cells in cold weather.

rows: Or Light ON/OFF for daytime only

content.body: The Smart BMS units include Bluetooth capability via the purchased Bluetooth module. Use the manufacturer BMS app (typically "Smart BMS" on iOS/Android) to monitor cell voltages, temperatures, current, and state of charge from a phone. The LEM HASS 200-S current sensor provides independent system-wide current measurement on the main bus — wire its output to an analog input on any monitoring device (ESP32, Arduino, Raspberry Pi).
A Raspberry Pi Zero W with an RS485-to-USB adapter can poll all three controllers on a shared Modbus bus using the epevermodbus Python library (~$50 total).

rows: Check cell balance. Inspect seals. Clean panels. Test all pumps.
Monitor battery temp weekly (<42°C). Verify fan cycling. Check solar harvest.
Test heater. Verify charge inhibit. Clean panels. Full health check.
Monitor heater cycling. Confirm charge inhibit. Winterize pumps if not needed. Battery sustains thermal alone for ~11.6 days with 4S3P.
